seo.title: 100 Security for AI Apps resources for developers
seo.description: A curated set of practical Security for AI Apps resources for engineers.

content.intro: Securing AI applications requires a shift from traditional perimeter defense to mitigating probabilistic risks such as prompt injection, data leakage through model outputs, and unauthorized API usage. This resource guide provides a curated list of tools and frameworks specifically designed to address the OWASP LLM Top 10 vulnerabilities and secure the infrastructure surrounding large language models.

items.title: Rebuff
items.description: An open-source multi-layered self-hosted prompt injection detector that uses heuristics, vector lookups, and LLM-based analysis.

items.title: NeMo Guardrails
items.description: NVIDIA's toolkit for defining programmable guardrails using Colang to ensure LLM interactions stay within predefined safety boundaries.

items.title: Guardrails AI
items.description: A framework for adding structural, type, and quality validation to LLM outputs using Pydantic-style schemas.

items.title: Lakera Guard
items.description: An enterprise-grade API for real-time protection against prompt injection, jailbreaking, and PII leakage.

items.title: Promptfoo
items.description: A CLI tool and library for testing prompt quality and security by running test cases against multiple models simultaneously.

items.title: Garak
items.description: An LLM vulnerability scanner that probes models for known weaknesses including hallucination, jailbreaks, and data extraction.

items.title: PyRIT
items.description: Microsoft's Python Risk Identification Tool for red teaming generative AI systems to identify security and fairness risks.

items.title: LLM-Security-Scanner
items.description: A lightweight tool for scanning prompt templates for common injection patterns before deployment.

items.title: Prompt Armor
items.description: A security proxy layer that sits between your application and the LLM provider to filter malicious payloads.

items.title: Vigil
items.description: A security scanner for LLM prompts that detects prompt injection and jailbreak attempts using a signature-based approach.

items.title: Microsoft Presidio
items.description: An open-source data protection SDK that provides PII identification and anonymization for text and images.

items.title: LLM Guard
items.description: A comprehensive tool for both input and output sanitization, including PII detection, toxicity filtering, and secret detection.

items.title: Private AI
items.description: A high-accuracy PII redaction API that supports over 50 languages and can be deployed on-premise to maintain data residency.

items.title: Skyflow
items.description: A data privacy vault that allows developers to isolate, protect, and govern sensitive customer data used in LLM workflows.

items.title: LangChain Privacy Filters
items.description: Built-in document transformers for LangChain to scrub sensitive data from documents before they are indexed in vector stores.

items.title: Cape Privacy
items.description: An API for encrypted LLM processing that ensures the model provider never sees the raw underlying data.

items.title: IronCore Labs Cloak
items.description: Transparent application-layer encryption for data stored in vector databases like Pinecone or Weaviate.

items.title: AWS Bedrock Guardrails
items.description: Managed service to filter toxic content and PII from inputs and outputs specifically for models hosted on AWS Bedrock.

items.title: Hugging Face SafeTensors
items.description: A simple, fast, and safe file format for storing tensors that prevents arbitrary code execution during model loading.

items.title: Anonymized RAG
items.description: Implementation pattern using hashing or tokenization for document metadata to prevent LLM reconstruction of user identities.

items.title: HashiCorp Vault LLM Engine
items.description: Manage and rotate LLM API keys dynamically to prevent long-lived credential exposure in environment variables.

items.title: AWS KMS Envelope Encryption
items.description: Encrypting high-dimensional vectors in databases using unique keys to ensure data at rest is unreadable without specific IAM roles.

items.title: Cloudflare Gateway for AI
items.description: An egress proxy that provides visibility, rate limiting, and security filtering for all outbound LLM API requests.

items.title: Pinecone RBAC
items.description: Role-Based Access Control for vector namespaces to ensure users only query segments of the vector database they are authorized to access.

items.title: LangSmith Audit Logs
items.description: Detailed tracing and logging of all LLM inputs and outputs to provide an audit trail for compliance and security forensics.

items.title: Snyk Code for AI
items.description: Static analysis tool configured to detect insecure patterns in LLM orchestration code, such as unsanitized prompt templates.

items.title: Tailscale for GPU Clusters
items.description: Zero-trust networking to secure communications between application servers and private self-hosted GPU inference nodes.

items.title: Istio Egress Gateways
items.description: Restricting LLM application pods to only communicate with verified model provider endpoints (e.g., api.openai.com).

items.title: GitHub Secret Scanning
items.description: Automated detection of leaked OpenAI, Anthropic, and Hugging Face tokens within private and public repositories.

items.title: Docker Scout
items.description: Vulnerability scanning for container images containing AI runtimes and libraries like PyTorch or TensorFlow.

pro_tips: Use XML or Markdown delimiters in your system prompt to clearly separate 'System Instructions' from 'User Input' to reduce injection success.
Implement 'Human-in-the-loop' approvals for any LLM-generated action that involves destructive operations or external data modification.
Set strict max_token limits on user inputs to prevent 'denial of wallet' attacks or model timeouts through massive payload injections.
Never store raw API keys in your code; use OIDC-based authentication (like GitHub Actions to AWS) to fetch short-lived credentials for LLM access.
Perform periodic 'Red Teaming' using automated tools like Garak to ensure that new model versions or prompt changes haven't introduced regressions.
